key_findings_summary: Verb-level misalignment between ILOs and assessments is the most common invisible failure — 'understand' masks multiple cognitive levels
Constructive Alignment breaks at three interfaces: ILO→TLA, ILO→AT, TLA→AT, with ILO→AT mismatch being most damaging due to backwash effect
Active learning improves outcomes 1.5x but lecture remains essential for L1-L2 content; the key is blended ratio shifting from lecture-heavy to active-heavy across learner progression
Group discussions fail for three predictable reasons: no accountability, vague prompts, no structured output — all fixable with design patterns
Flipped classroom compliance is the main bottleneck; requires all three: low volume, scaffolded guide, graded consequence
UDL's three principles (Engagement, Representation, Action & Expression) are implementable at zero cost via multi-modal switching, choice boards, and Bloom-level questioning
Differentiation's core pattern: standardize outcomes (what), differentiate pathways (how). 'Core + Choice' framework with weekly convergence checkpoints
Teacher time budget is the hardest constraint for feedback; the solution is designing feedback systems (rubrics + peer review + rotation) not performing feedback labor
Diagnostic pre-assessment with 3-tier grouping is the most scalable differentiation entry point
complementary_patterns: R1's Three-Gate Alignment Audit + R3's Activity Selection Matrix form a natural sequence: align objectives first, then choose activities by objective level
R4's 'core + choice' framework reinforces R3's breadth-vs-depth decision rule — both recommend compressing breadth for depth when constrained
R3's compliance-first rule for flipped classrooms and R4's 'check-feedback-revise cycle' both depend on low-stakes formative assessment mechanisms
All three reports converge on: assessment drives student behavior more than stated objectives (backwash effect), making assessment design the highest-leverage intervention point
coverage_gaps: R2 (cognitive load & sequencing) was lost — coverage on threshold concepts, content chunking, and spiral sequencing is missing
No report specifically addresses digital/online course design modalities vs in-person
No report addresses course evaluation methods (beyond student feedback loops)
